does: This paper establishes pre-training as the essential prerequisite for weak-to-strong generalization, where a strong model learns from weak supervision and surpasses the weak supervisor. The authors formalize the problem within a high-dimensional single-index model framework using spiked Gaussian data, modeling pre-training as spectral initialization. They prove that weak-to-strong generalization becomes achievable when pre-training provides geometric warm-start conditions that place the model in an effective region with perturbed strong-convexity. The paper derives a rigorous generalization bound capturing the optimization dynamics: initial performance improvement followed by saturation constrained by the weak supervisor's bias. These theoretical predictions are validated through experiments confirming the key assumptions and insights underlying their analysis.
connects: This work extends the weak-to-strong generalization paradigm, which has emerged as a framework for aligning powerful AI systems with imperfect human feedback. The paper builds on recent impossibility results showing that learning fails under random initialization, contributing theoretical understanding to why pre-training fundamentally changes learnability. It connects to classical statistical learning theory and modern high-dimensional analysis, particularly single-index models and spectral methods in machine learning. The work also relates to broader literature on transfer learning and feature learning, where pre-training on related tasks provides useful representations. Additionally, it engages with recent work on the gap between weak supervision and strong model capability, addressing a practically important scenario where the training signal limits model performance.
enables: This theoretical characterization opens directions for designing better pre-training schemes specifically optimized for weak-to-strong generalization settings. The results suggest methods for verifying when a pre-trained model can provably escape weak supervision and identifying the minimal pre-training requirements needed. The framework enables investigation of how pre-training quality, weak supervisor strength, and model capacity interact in determining achievable performance. Future work could extend the analysis beyond Gaussian settings to more realistic data distributions, develop practical algorithms that exploit the effective region characterization, and study multi-task or continual learning scenarios where weak-to-strong generalization persists across multiple weak supervisors, with applications to human-AI collaboration and AI safety.
